Summary: In macrophages, PU.1-bound regions were significantly co-enriched for motifs of macrophage-restricted, lineage-determining transcription factors, and this enrichment was similar in promoter-proximal and promoter-distal regions. Spatial analysis showed C/EBP and AP-1 motifs were highly enriched within 100 bp of macrophage-restricted distal PU.1 sites. Secondary PU.1 motifs were enriched within 100 bp of the central PU.1 motif, particularly at macrophage-specific and commonly bound sites. In PU1−/− and tamoxifen-treated PUER cells, PU.1 determines LXRβ recruitment to binding sites located within 100 bp of PU.1. This PU.1-dependent recruitment was accompanied by a PU.1-dependent gain in H3K4me1 signal at macrophage-specific LXRβ-bound regions. In LXRα/β double-knockout bone marrow-derived macrophages, LXRs did not alter the PU.1 cistrome or the H3K4me1 pattern at these regions. PU.1 promotes C/EBPβ binding in myeloid lineage cells. In the absence of PU.1, C/EBPβ showed reduced genome-wide binding and lost numerous sites that correspond to PU.1-bound locations in primary macrophages, including at the macrophage-specific CD14 gene. Motif analysis of C/EBPβ-bound regions in PU.1−/− cells recovered C/EBP, C/EBP:AP-1, AP-1, RUNX and a non-PU.1 Ets motif and did not recover the PU.1 binding motif found in primary macrophages.
Reference: Heinz, S., Benner, C., Spann, N., Bertolino, E., Lin, Y. C., Laslo, P., Cheng, J. X., Murre, C., Singh, H., & Glass, C. K. (2010). Simple combinations of lineage-determining transcription factors prime cis-regulatory elements required for macrophage and B cell identities. Mol Cell, 38(4), 576-589. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2010.05.004

Summary: In RA, PU.1 could promote the development of arthritis by directly targeting FMS-like tyrosine kinase 3 (FLT3) in macrophages and fibroblast-like synoviocytes (FLS). In vitro, siRNA knockdown of PU.1 in murine RAW264.7 macrophages suppressed inflammatory features and reduced the M1/M2 ratio and inflammatory cytokine expression. In contrast, PU.1 knockdown in primary monocyte-derived M0 macrophages from RA patients increased pro-inflammatory markers and repressed anti-inflammatory markers. FLT3 was identified as a functional PU.1 target because FLT3 knockdown produced effects opposite to PU.1 knockdown in mouse and human macrophages. Taken together, the PU.1–FLT3 axis is described as an essential proinflammatory axis in RA that promotes inflammatory features in macrophages. Macrophage-specific (LysM-cre) PU.1 knockout mice have been established; macrophage-specific (LysM-cre) FLT3 mice could provide a better mouse model to evaluate the effect of the PU.1–FLT3 axis on macrophages in the pathogenesis of mouse arthritis and to determine which component of the PU.1–FLT3 axis predominantly drives macrophage effects. Primary synovial macrophages from RA patients will be cultured to test whether the PU.1–FLT3 promotive effect on RA is conserved between humans and mice. Single-cell sequencing of joint synovium from the mice will assess overall cellular changes and further evaluate the PU.1–FLT3 axis.
Reference: Tu, J., Chen, W., Fang, Y., Han, D., Chen, Y., Jiang, H., Tan, X., Xu, Z., Wu, X., Wang, H., Zhu, X., Hong, W., Li, Z., Zhu, C., Wang, X., & Wei, W. (2023). PU.1 promotes development of rheumatoid arthritis via repressing FLT3 in macrophages and fibroblast-like synoviocytes. Ann Rheum Dis, 82(2), 198-211. https://doi.org/10.1136/ard-2022-222708

Summary: PU.1 is an ETS-family transcription factor implicated in hematopoiesis. PU.1 functions include generation of early myeloid progenitors, fate choice between granulocytic and monocytic lineages of neutrophil‑macrophage progenitors, and maintenance of macrophage‑specific gene accessibility during monocytic differentiation. Mechanistic studies identify PU.1 as a direct target of Zbtb14. In zebrafish zbtb14-deficient embryos and larvae, pu.1 expression increased in mpeg1.1+ macrophage-lineage cells. Injection of a dominant‑negative PU.1 driven by the mpeg1.1 promoter rescued aberrant macrophage expansion in zbtb14 mutants. The development of neutrophil lineage remained normal in zbtb14 mutants. Overexpression of full‑length pu.1 in wild-type embryos expanded macrophage numbers without affecting neutrophils. A pu.1 Δ371 mutant with reduced transactivation showed reduced macrophages. zbtb14-/-; pu.1 Δ371 double mutants showed no alleviation, indicating zbtb14 is epistatic to pu.1. Zbtb14 transcripts were consistently higher in mpeg1.1+ than in mpx+ cells.
Reference: Deng, Y., Wang, H., Liu, X., Yuan, H., Xu, J., de Thé, H., Zhou, J., & Zhu, J. (2022). Zbtb14 regulates monocyte and macrophage development through inhibiting pu.1 expression in zebrafish. Elife, 11, e80760. https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.80760

Summary: In the context of PU.1, CX3CR1– perivascular macrophages express CX3CR1 during their ontogeny and subsequently lose that expression. The Spi1 GFP/GFP (PU.1) and Cx3cr1-Cre models showed that CX3CR1– perivascular macrophages most likely derive from the same yolk-sac-derived progenitor lineage as the broader perivascular macrophage population.
Reference: Siret, C., van Lessen, M., Bavais, J., Jeong, H. W., Reddy, S. a. m. a. w. a. r. S. K., Kapupara, K., Wang, S., Simic, M., de Fabritus, L., Tchoghandjian, A., Fallet, M., Huang, H., Sarrazin, S., Sieweke, M. H., Stumm, R., Sorokin, L., Adams, R. H., Schulte-Merker, S., Kiefer, F., & van de Pavert, S. A. (2022). Deciphering the heterogeneity of the Lyve1+ perivascular macrophages in the mouse brain. Nat Commun, 13(1), 7366. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-35166-9

Summary: PU.1/SPI1 acts as a master regulator of macrophage and microglia development in mammals and teleosts. PU.1 controls expression of genes required for macrophage and microglia development. PU.1 loss or downregulation is associated with reduced Csf1r and loss of anti‑apoptotic genes such as BCL2A1. Genetic disruption of PU.1 causes early embryonic lethality and absence of macrophages including microglia in mice. Pu.1‑null zebrafish mutants block macrophage and microglia development at early stages. Conditional ablation of PU.1 in mice or Pu.1/Spi‑b in zebrafish produces rapid microglial depletion, and tamoxifen‑induced PU.1 inactivation in CK‑p25 mice produced a reduction in microglia at young ages. SPI1 expression remains high in microglia and ChIP‑Seq in BV2 cells identified many PU.1 targets. siRNA silencing of PU.1 in human glial culture reduced microglial viability and phagocytosis of amyloid‑beta. ER‑fusion inactivation of one or two SPI1 copies did not affect alveolar macrophage viability. In an AD mouse model, conditional or pharmacological PU.1 inhibition ameliorated neuroinflammation and prevented microgliosis. PU.1 inhibition improved cognition in that model. Whether PU.1 directly controls microglial survival in vivo remains unresolved.
Reference: Wu, Y., Guo, W., Kuang, H., Wu, X., Trinh, T. H., Wang, Y., Zhao, S., Wen, Z., & Yu, T. (2025). Pu.1/Spi1 dosage controls the turnover and maintenance of microglia in zebrafish and mammals. Elife, 14, RP105788. https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.105788
